summary: I was born in Melrose, New Mexico, on December 8, 1910. Although I left when I was about two years old, Melrose is where my story begins. It’s a small place, but it’s my birthplace and part of my early roots.

Many of the foster children we raised ended up scattered all over the country, including places like Washington state. We still keep in touch with about twelve of them, no matter where they are, including those in Washington. It’s nice to have that extended family spread out but connected.

Some of the foster children we raised live in Modesto, California. Along with others in places like Washington and Sacramento, we maintain close contact with many of them. We often get together for holidays, which keeps those family ties strong despite the distance.

Sacramento is another place where some of our foster children settled. We’ve managed to keep close relationships with about a dozen of them, including those in Sacramento. We make it a point to gather for Christmas and Thanksgiving, which helps keep the family bond alive.

After leaving New Mexico when I was about two, my family moved to Texas, where I lived until I was around eight years old. Texas was my home during those early childhood years before we moved to California. It was an important part of my early life journey.

I went to grammar school and high school in Santa Cruz, California. While in high school, I played multiple sports and even worked on the boardwalk, where I met Pappy Smith, who had a concession there. Santa Cruz was a formative place for me before heading to the University of Nevada.

I attended the University of Nevada, Reno, from 1930 to 1934 on a football scholarship. I played halfback and quarterback on the football team and also participated in freshman basketball under Coach Philbrook. The university was where I developed my athletic skills and met many influential people like Jake Lawlor and Tommy Hill.

Rabbit Bradshaw was one of my high school coaches in Santa Cruz, and he was quite famous for becoming an All American at Nevada. He kept encouraging me to come to Nevada, which I did in 1930 on a football scholarship. His influence was a big part of my decision to attend the University of Nevada.

I met Pappy Smith back when I was still in high school working on the Santa Cruz boardwalk. He had a concession there, and I worked a soft drink stand and helped with events. I got to know him well before he eventually came to Reno, and we also crossed paths at various carnivals and fairs.

Although I didn’t work directly at Harolds Club, I knew Pappy Smith before he came to Reno and became associated with Harolds Club. Our connection was through the Santa Cruz boardwalk and carnival circuits before his time there. Harolds Club was part of the Reno scene that Pappy became known for.

Philbrook was my football coach at the University of Nevada and also coached the freshman basketball team when I played. He came from Notre Dame and was quite a respected figure in our athletic programs. Under his guidance, I played both football and basketball during my early college years.

Philbrook, who coached me in football and basketball at the University of Nevada, was originally from the University of Notre Dame. His background brought a high level of coaching expertise to our small school. Notre Dame’s influence came to us through him during the early 1930s.

Doc Martie was the varsity basketball coach at the University of Nevada when I was a freshman. He was a very colorful character and known for producing some of the finest college teams for a small school. I played against players like Jake Lawlor under his coaching during those years.

Jake Lawlor was a player I competed against as a freshman basketball player at the University of Nevada. He was also involved in the fraternity baseball league along with Tommy Hill, Oscar Freitag, and the Peccole family. We were all at the university around the same time in the early 1930s.

Jack Threlkel managed a very fast semi-pro baseball team that I played for during the early 1930s, especially around 1933 and 1934. Playing for his team was a big part of my baseball experience outside of the university’s inter-fraternity league. It was a competitive and exciting time for me in baseball.

Lovelock was one of the towns where I played summer baseball outside of the university leagues. Along with Fallon and other places, it was part of the Sagebrush League, which was a very fast and competitive summer baseball league in Nevada. Playing in Lovelock helped me stay sharp during the off-season.

Fallon was another town where I played summer baseball with teams in the Sagebrush League. The league was quite competitive and included players from the university and surrounding areas. Playing in Fallon and other towns kept me active in baseball beyond the school year.

The Sagebrush League was a fast and competitive summer baseball league in Nevada during the early 1930s. I played in this league with teams from towns like Lovelock and Fallon. It was a great way to continue playing baseball outside of the university’s inter-fraternity league and helped me develop my skills.

Tommy Hill was one of the players in the fraternity baseball league at the University of Nevada along with Jake Lawlor, Oscar Freitag, and the Peccole family. We were all part of the university community in the early 1930s and played competitive baseball together. He was a familiar face in the athletic circles I moved in.

Oscar Freitag played in the fraternity baseball league at the University of Nevada during the early 1930s. Alongside players like Jake Lawlor, Tommy Hill, and the Peccole family, he was part of the competitive baseball scene on campus. We all shared the experience of playing in that league during our college years.

The Peccole family was involved in the fraternity baseball league at the University of Nevada while I was there in the early 1930s. They played alongside other notable players like Jake Lawlor, Tommy Hill, and Oscar Freitag. Their participation helped make the league competitive and memorable.

During my time playing basketball at the University of Nevada, we competed against teams like St. Mary's College of California. They were part of the regional competition that included other California schools. Playing against them was part of the challenge and excitement of college basketball back then.

Santa Clara University was one of the teams we played against in basketball during my college years at the University of Nevada. Competing with them and other California schools like St. Mary's and San Jose added to the level of play and experience. Those games were held in the Old Gym, which was our basketball venue.

San Jose was another California city where the University of Nevada basketball team competed during my time there. Along with teams from St. Mary's, Santa Clara, Fresno, and San Francisco, we faced a strong regional schedule. These games were important highlights of our basketball seasons in the early 1930s.

Fresno was one of the California cities where our basketball team at the University of Nevada played during the early 1930s. We competed against schools from Fresno and other parts of California, which made for a challenging and exciting schedule. These games helped build our reputation as a competitive small school team.

San Francisco was included in the list of California cities where we played basketball during my college years at the University of Nevada. Facing teams from San Francisco and other regional schools was part of the competitive atmosphere we enjoyed. Our games were mostly held in the Old Gym on campus.
